NKJV.1: Receive one who is weak in the faith, but not to disputes over doubtful things.
NKJV.2: For one believes he may eat all things, but he who is weak eats only vegetables.
NKJV.3: Let not him who eats despise him who does not eat, and let not him who does not eat judge him who eats; for God has received him.
NKJV.4: Who are you to judge another’s servant? To his own master he stands or falls. Indeed, he will be made to stand, for God is able to make him stand.
NKJV.5: One person esteems one day above another; another esteems every day alike. Let each be fully convinced in his own mind.
NKJV.6: He who observes the day, observes it to the Lord; and he who does not observe the day, to the Lord he does not observe it. He who eats, eats to the Lord, for he gives God thanks; and he who does not eat, to the Lord he does not eat, and gives God thanks.
NKJV.7: For none of us lives to himself, and no one dies to himself.
NKJV.8: For if we live, we live to the Lord; and if we die, we die to the Lord. Therefore, whether we live or die, we are the Lord’s.
NKJV.9: For to this end Christ died and rose and lived again, that He might be Lord of both the dead and the living.
NKJV.10: But why do you judge your brother? Or why do you show contempt for your brother? For we shall all stand before the judgment seat of Christ.
NKJV.11: For it is written: “As I live, says the LORD, Every knee shall bow to Me, And every tongue shall confess to God.”
NKJV.12: So then each of us shall give account of himself to God.
NKJV.13: Therefore let us not judge one another anymore, but rather resolve this, not to put a stumbling block or a cause to fall in our brother’s way.
NKJV.14: I know and am convinced by the Lord Jesus that there is nothing unclean of itself; but to him who considers anything to be unclean, to him it is unclean.
NKJV.15: Yet if your brother is grieved because of your food, you are no longer walking in love. Do not destroy with your food the one for whom Christ died.
NKJV.16: Therefore do not let your good be spoken of as evil;
NKJV.17: for the kingdom of God is not eating and drinking, but righteousness and peace and joy in the Holy Spirit.
NKJV.18: For he who serves Christ in these things is acceptable to God and approved by men.
NKJV.19: Therefore let us pursue the things which make for peace and the things by which one may edify another.
NKJV.20: Do not destroy the work of God for the sake of food. All things indeed are pure, but it is evil for the man who eats with offense.
NKJV.21: It is good neither to eat meat nor drink wine nor do anything by which your brother stumbles or is offended or is made weak.
NKJV.22: Do you have faith? Have it to yourself before God. Happy is he who does not condemn himself in what he approves.
NKJV.23: But he who doubts is condemned if he eats, because he does not eat from faith; for whatever is not from faith is sin.

ESV.1: As for the one who is weak in faith, welcome him, but not to quarrel over opinions.
ESV.2: One person believes he may eat anything, while the weak person eats only vegetables.
ESV.3: Let not the one who eats despise the one who abstains, and let not the one who abstains pass judgment on the one who eats, for God has welcomed him.
ESV.4: Who are you to pass judgment on the servant of another? It is before his own master  that he stands or falls. And he will be upheld, for the Lord is able to make him stand.
ESV.5: One person esteems one day as better than another, while another esteems all days alike. Each one should be fully convinced in his own mind.
ESV.6: The one who observes the day, observes it in honor of the Lord. The one who eats, eats in honor of the Lord, since he gives thanks to God, while the one who abstains, abstains in honor of the Lord and gives thanks to God.
ESV.7: For none of us lives to himself, and none of us dies to himself.
ESV.8: For if we live, we live to the Lord, and if we die, we die to the Lord. So then, whether we live or whether we die, we are the Lord's.
ESV.9: For to this end Christ died and lived again, that he might be Lord both of the dead and of the living.
ESV.10: Why do you pass judgment on your brother? Or you, why do you despise your brother? For we will all stand before the judgment seat of God;
ESV.11: for it is written, “As I live, says the Lord, every knee shall bow to me, and every tongue shall confess  to God.”
ESV.12: So then each of us will give an account of himself to God.
ESV.13: Therefore let us not pass judgment on one another any longer, but rather decide never to put a stumbling block or hindrance in the way of a brother.
ESV.14: I know and am persuaded in the Lord Jesus that nothing is unclean in itself, but it is unclean for anyone who thinks it unclean.
ESV.15: For if your brother is grieved by what you eat, you are no longer walking in love. By what you eat, do not destroy the one for whom Christ died.
ESV.16: So do not let what you regard as good be spoken of as evil.
ESV.17: For the kingdom of God is not a matter of eating and drinking but of righteousness and peace and joy in the Holy Spirit.
ESV.18: Whoever thus serves Christ is acceptable to God and approved by men.
ESV.19: So then let us pursue what makes for peace and for mutual upbuilding.
ESV.20: Do not, for the sake of food, destroy the work of God. Everything is indeed clean, but it is wrong for anyone to make another stumble by what he eats.
ESV.21: It is good not to eat meat or drink wine or do anything that causes your brother to stumble.
ESV.22: The faith that you have, keep between yourself and God. Blessed is the one who has no reason to pass judgment on himself for what he approves.
ESV.23: But whoever has doubts is condemned if he eats, because the eating is not from faith. For whatever does not proceed from faith is sin.

NASB.1: Now accept the one who is weak in faith, [but] not for [the purpose of] passing judgment on his opinions.
NASB.2: One person has faith that he may eat all things, but he who is weak eats vegetables [only].
NASB.3: The one who eats is not to regard with contempt the one who does not eat, and the one who does not eat is not to judge the one who eats, for God has accepted him.
NASB.4: Who are you to judge the servant of another? To his own master he stands or falls; and he will stand, for the Lord is able to make him stand.
NASB.5: One person regards one day above another, another regards every day [alike]. Each person must be fully convinced in his own mind.
NASB.6: He who observes the day, observes it for the Lord, and he who eats, does so for the Lord, for he gives thanks to God; and he who eats not, for the Lord he does not eat, and gives thanks to God.
NASB.7: For not one of us lives for himself, and not one dies for himself;
NASB.8: for if we live, we live for the Lord, or if we die, we die for the Lord; therefore whether we live or die, we are the Lord's.
NASB.9: For to this end Christ died and lived again, that He might be Lord both of the dead and of the living.
NASB.10: But you, why do you judge your brother? Or you again, why do you regard your brother with contempt? For we will all stand before the judgment seat of God.
NASB.11: For it is written, 'AS I LIVE, SAYS THE LORD, EVERY KNEE SHALL BOW TO ME, AND EVERY TONGUE SHALL GIVE PRAISE TO GOD.'
NASB.12: So then each one of us will give an account of himself to God.
NASB.13: Therefore let us not judge one another anymore, but rather determine this-- not to put an obstacle or a stumbling block in a brother's way.
NASB.14: I know and am convinced in the Lord Jesus that nothing is unclean in itself; but to him who thinks anything to be unclean, to him it is unclean.
NASB.15: For if because of food your brother is hurt, you are no longer walking according to love. Do not destroy with your food him for whom Christ died.
NASB.16: Therefore do not let what is for you a good thing be spoken of as evil;
NASB.17: for the kingdom of God is not eating and drinking, but righteousness and peace and joy in the Holy Spirit.
NASB.18: For he who in this [way] serves Christ is acceptable to God and approved by men.
NASB.19: So then we pursue the things which make for peace and the building up of one another.
NASB.20: Do not tear down the work of God for the sake of food. All things indeed are clean, but they are evil for the man who eats and gives offense.
NASB.21: It is good not to eat meat or to drink wine, or [to do anything] by which your brother stumbles.
NASB.22: The faith which you have, have as your own conviction before God. Happy is he who does not condemn himself in what he approves.
NASB.23: But he who doubts is condemned if he eats, because [his eating is] not from faith; and whatever is not from faith is sin.

NLT.1: Accept other believers who are weak in faith, and don’t argue with them about what they think is right or wrong.
NLT.2: For instance, one person believes it’s all right to eat anything. But another believer with a sensitive conscience will eat only vegetables.
NLT.3: Those who feel free to eat anything must not look down on those who don’t. And those who don’t eat certain foods must not condemn those who do, for God has accepted them.
NLT.4: Who are you to condemn someone else’s servants? Their own master will judge whether they stand or fall. And with the Lord’s help, they will stand and receive his approval.
NLT.5: In the same way, some think one day is more holy than another day, while others think every day is alike. You should each be fully convinced that whichever day you choose is acceptable.
NLT.6: Those who worship the Lord on a special day do it to honor him. Those who eat any kind of food do so to honor the Lord, since they give thanks to God before eating. And those who refuse to eat certain foods also want to please the Lord and give thanks to God.
NLT.7: For we don’t live for ourselves or die for ourselves.
NLT.8: If we live, it’s to honor the Lord. And if we die, it’s to honor the Lord. So whether we live or die, we belong to the Lord.
NLT.9: Christ died and rose again for this very purpose — to be Lord both of the living and of the dead.
NLT.10: So why do you condemn another believer ? Why do you look down on another believer? Remember, we will all stand before the judgment seat of God.
NLT.11:
For the Scriptures say, “‘As surely as I live,’ says the LORD,
‘every knee will bend to me,
and every tongue will declare allegiance to God. ’”
NLT.12: Yes, each of us will give a personal account to God.
NLT.13: So let’s stop condemning each other. Decide instead to live in such a way that you will not cause another believer to stumble and fall.
NLT.14: I know and am convinced on the authority of the Lord Jesus that no food, in and of itself, is wrong to eat. But if someone believes it is wrong, then for that person it is wrong.
NLT.15: And if another believer is distressed by what you eat, you are not acting in love if you eat it. Don’t let your eating ruin someone for whom Christ died.
NLT.16: Then you will not be criticized for doing something you believe is good.
NLT.17: For the Kingdom of God is not a matter of what we eat or drink, but of living a life of goodness and peace and joy in the Holy Spirit.
NLT.18: If you serve Christ with this attitude, you will please God, and others will approve of you, too.
NLT.19: So then, let us aim for harmony in the church and try to build each other up.
NLT.20: Don’t tear apart the work of God over what you eat. Remember, all foods are acceptable, but it is wrong to eat something if it makes another person stumble.
NLT.21: It is better not to eat meat or drink wine or do anything else if it might cause another believer to stumble.
NLT.22: You may believe there’s nothing wrong with what you are doing, but keep it between yourself and God. Blessed are those who don’t feel guilty for doing something they have decided is right.
NLT.23: But if you have doubts about whether or not you should eat something, you are sinning if you go ahead and do it. For you are not following your convictions. If you do anything you believe is not right, you are sinning.

WEB.1: Now accept one who is weak in faith, but not for disputes over opinions.
WEB.2: One man has faith to eat all things, but he who is weak eats only vegetables.
WEB.3: Don’t let him who eats despise him who doesn’t eat. Don’t let him who doesn’t eat judge him who eats, for God has accepted him.
WEB.4: Who are you who judge another’s servant? To his own lord he stands or falls. Yes, he will be made to stand, for God has power to make him stand.
WEB.5: One man esteems one day as more important. Another esteems every day alike. Let each man be fully assured in his own mind.
WEB.6: He who observes the day, observes it to the Lord; and he who does not observe the day, to the Lord he does not observe it. He who eats, eats to the Lord, for he gives God thanks. He who doesn’t eat, to the Lord he doesn’t eat, and gives God thanks.
WEB.7: For none of us lives to himself, and none dies to himself.
WEB.8: For if we live, we live to the Lord. Or if we die, we die to the Lord. If therefore we live or die, we are the Lord’s.
WEB.9: For to this end Christ died, rose, and lived again, that he might be Lord of both the dead and the living.
WEB.10: But you, why do you judge your brother? Or you again, why do you despise your brother? For we will all stand before the judgment seat of Christ.
WEB.11: For it is written, “‘As I live,’ says the Lord, ‘to me every knee will bow. Every tongue will confess to God.’”
WEB.12: So then each one of us will give account of himself to God.
WEB.13: Therefore let’s not judge one another any more, but judge this rather, that no man put a stumbling block in his brother’s way, or an occasion for falling.
WEB.14: I know, and am persuaded in the Lord Jesus, that nothing is unclean of itself; except that to him who considers anything to be unclean, to him it is unclean.
WEB.15: Yet if because of food your brother is grieved, you walk no longer in love. Don’t destroy with your food him for whom Christ died.
WEB.16: Then don’t let your good be slandered,
WEB.17: for God’s Kingdom is not eating and drinking, but righteousness, peace, and joy in the Holy Spirit.
WEB.18: For he who serves Christ in these things is acceptable to God and approved by men.
WEB.19: So then, let us follow after things which make for peace, and things by which we may build one another up.
WEB.20: Don’t overthrow God’s work for food’s sake. All things indeed are clean, however it is evil for that man who creates a stumbling block by eating.
WEB.21: It is good to not eat meat, drink wine, nor do anything by which your brother stumbles, is offended, or is made weak.
WEB.22: Do you have faith? Have it to yourself before God. Happy is he who doesn’t judge himself in that which he approves.
WEB.23: But he who doubts is condemned if he eats, because it isn’t of faith; and whatever is not of faith is sin.
WEB.24: Now to him who is able to establish you according to my Good News and the preaching of Jesus Christ, according to the revelation of the mystery which has been kept secret through long ages,
WEB.25: but now is revealed, and by the Scriptures of the prophets, according to the commandment of the eternal God, is made known for obedience of faith to all the nations;
WEB.26: to the only wise God, through Jesus Christ, to whom be the glory forever! Amen.

CSB17.1: Accept <sup>ⓘ</sup> anyone who is weak in faith, <sup>ⓙ</sup> but don’t argue about disputed matters.
CSB17.2: One person believes he may eat anything, <sup>ⓚ</sup> while one who is weak eats only vegetables.
CSB17.3: One who eats must not look down on one who does not eat, <sup>ⓛ</sup> and one who does not eat must not judge one who does, <sup>ⓜ</sup> because God has accepted him. <sup>ⓝ</sup>
CSB17.4: Who are you to judge <sup>ⓐ</sup> another’s household servant? Before his own Lord he stands or falls. And he will stand, because the Lord is able <sup>[1]</sup> to make him stand.
CSB17.5: One person judges one day to be more important than another day. <sup>ⓑ</sup> Someone else judges every day to be the same. Let each one be fully convinced in his own mind. <sup>ⓒ</sup>
CSB17.6: Whoever observes the day, observes it for the honor of the Lord. <sup>[2]</sup> Whoever eats, eats for the Lord, since he gives thanks to God; <sup>ⓓ</sup> and whoever does not eat, it is for the Lord that he does not eat it, and he gives thanks to God.
CSB17.7: For none of us lives for himself, and no one dies for himself. <sup>ⓔ</sup>
CSB17.8: If we live, we live for the Lord; and if we die, we die for the Lord. Therefore, whether we live or die, we belong to the Lord. <sup>ⓕ</sup>
CSB17.9: Christ died and returned to life <sup>ⓖ</sup> for this: that he might be Lord over both the dead and the living. <sup>ⓗ</sup>
CSB17.10: But you, why do you judge your brother or sister? Or you, why do you despise your brother or sister? For we will all stand before the judgment seat of God. <sup>[3]</sup> <sup>ⓘ</sup>
CSB17.11:
For it is written, As I live, says the Lord,
every knee will bow to me, ⓙ
and every tongue will give praise to God. [4] ⓚ
CSB17.12: So then, each of us will give an account of himself to God. <sup>ⓛ</sup>
CSB17.13: Therefore, let us no longer judge one another. <sup>ⓜ</sup> Instead decide never to put a stumbling block or pitfall in the way of your brother or sister. <sup>ⓝ</sup>
CSB17.14: I know and am persuaded in the Lord Jesus that nothing is unclean in itself. <sup>ⓐ</sup> Still, to someone who considers a thing to be unclean, to that one it is unclean. <sup>ⓑ</sup>
CSB17.15: For if your brother or sister is hurt by what you eat, you are no longer walking according to love. <sup>ⓒ</sup> Do not destroy, by what you eat, someone for whom Christ died. <sup>ⓓ</sup>
CSB17.16: Therefore, do not let your good be slandered, <sup>ⓔ</sup>
CSB17.17: for the kingdom of God is not eating and drinking, <sup>ⓕ</sup> but righteousness, peace, and joy <sup>ⓖ</sup> in the Holy Spirit.
CSB17.18: Whoever serves Christ <sup>ⓗ</sup> in this way is acceptable to God and receives human approval. <sup>ⓘ</sup>
CSB17.19: So then, let us pursue what promotes peace <sup>ⓙ</sup> and what builds up one another. <sup>ⓚ</sup>
CSB17.20: Do not tear down God’s work because of food. Everything is clean, but it is wrong to make someone fall by what he eats. <sup>ⓛ</sup>
CSB17.21: It is a good thing not to eat meat, or drink wine, or do anything that makes your brother or sister stumble. <sup>[5]</sup>
CSB17.22: Whatever you believe about these things, keep between yourself and God. Blessed is the one who does not condemn himself by what he approves. <sup>ⓜ</sup>
CSB17.23: But whoever doubts stands condemned if he eats, <sup>ⓝ</sup> because his eating is not from faith, <sup>[6]</sup> and everything that is not from faith is sin.

AMP.1: As for the one whose faith is weak, accept him [into your fellowship], but not for [the purpose of] quarreling over his opinions.
AMP.2: One man's faith permits him to eat everything, while the weak believer eats only vegetables [to avoid eating ritually unclean meat or something previously considered unclean].
AMP.3: The one who eats [everything] is not to look down on the one who does not eat, and the one who does not eat must not criticize or pass judgment on the one who eats [everything], for God has accepted him.
AMP.4: Who are you to judge the servant of another? Before his own master he stands [approved] or falls [out of favor]. And he [who serves the Master—the Lord] will stand, for the Lord is able to make him stand.
AMP.5: One person regards one day as better [or more important] than another, while another regards every day [the same as any other]. Let everyone be fully convinced (assured, satisfied) in his own mind.
AMP.6: He who observes the day, observes it for the Lord. He who eats, eats for the Lord, since he gives thanks to God; while he who abstains, abstains for the Lord and gives thanks to God.
AMP.7: None of us lives for himself [for his own benefit, but for the Lord], and none of us dies for himself [but for the Lord].
AMP.8: If we live, we live for the Lord, and if we die, we die for the Lord. So then, whether we live or die, we are the Lord's.
AMP.9: For Christ died and lived again for this reason, that He might be Lord of both the dead and the living.
AMP.10: But you, why do you criticize your brother? Or you again, why do you look down on your [believing] brother or regard him with contempt? For we will all stand before the judgment seat of God [who alone is judge].
AMP.11:
For it is written [in Scripture],
"As I live, says the Lord, every knee shall bow to Me,
And every tongue shall give praise to God."
AMP.12: So then, each of us will give an account of himself to God.
AMP.13: Then let us not criticize one another anymore, but rather determine this—not to put an obstacle or a stumbling block or a source of temptation in another believer's way.
AMP.14: I know and am convinced [as one] in the Lord Jesus that nothing is unclean [ritually defiled, and unholy] in itself; but [nonetheless] it is unclean to anyone who thinks it is unclean.
AMP.15: If your brother is being hurt or offended because of food [that you insist on eating], you are no longer walking in love [toward him]. Do not let what you eat destroy and spiritually harm one for whom Christ died.
AMP.16: Therefore do not let what is a good thing for you [because of your freedom to choose] be spoken of as evil [by someone else];
AMP.17: for the kingdom of God is not a matter of eating and drinking [what one likes], but of righteousness and peace and joy in the Holy Spirit.
AMP.18: For the one who serves Christ in this way [recognizing that food choice is secondary] is acceptable to God and is approved by men.
AMP.19: So then, let us pursue [with enthusiasm] the things which make for peace and the building up of one another [things which lead to spiritual growth].
AMP.20: Do not, for the sake of food, tear down the work of God. All things indeed are [ceremonially] clean, but they are wrong for the person who eats and offends [another's conscience in the process].
AMP.21: It is good [to do the right thing and] not eat meat or drink wine, or do anything that offends your brother and weakens him spiritually.
AMP.22: The faith which you have [that gives you freedom of choice], have as your own conviction before God [just keep it between yourself and God, seeking His will]. Happy is he who has no reason to condemn himself for what he approves.
AMP.23: But he who is uncertain [about eating a particular thing] is condemned if he eats, because he is not acting from faith. Whatever is not from faith is sin [whatever is done with doubt is sinful].

MSG.1: Welcome with open arms fellow believers who don't see things the way you do. And don't jump all over them every time they do or say something you don't agree with—even when it seems that they are strong on opinions but weak in the faith department. Remember, they have their own history to deal with. Treat them gently.
MSG.2: For instance, a person who has been around for a while might well be convinced that he can eat anything on the table, while another, with a different background, might assume all Christians should be vegetarians and eat accordingly.
MSG.3: But since both are guests at Christ's table, wouldn't it be terribly rude if they fell to criticizing what the other ate or didn't eat? God, after all, invited them both to the table.
MSG.4: Do you have any business crossing people off the guest list or interfering with God's welcome? If there are corrections to be made or manners to be learned, God can handle that without your help.
MSG.5: Or, say, one person thinks that some days should be set aside as holy and another thinks that each day is pretty much like any other. There are good reasons either way. So, each person is free to follow the convictions of conscience.
MSG.6: What's important in all this is that if you keep a holy day, keep it for God's sake; if you eat meat, eat it to the glory of God and thank God for prime rib; if you're a vegetarian, eat vegetables to the glory of God and thank God for broccoli.
MSG.7: None of us are permitted to insist on our own way in these matters.
MSG.8: It's God we are answerable to—all the way from life to death and everything in between—not each other.
MSG.9: That's why Jesus lived and died and then lived again: so that he could be our Master across the entire range of life and death, and free us from the petty tyrannies of each other.
MSG.10: So where does that leave you when you criticize a brother? And where does that leave you when you condescend to a sister? I'd say it leaves you looking pretty silly—or worse. Eventually, we're all going to end up kneeling side by side in the place of judgment, facing God. Your critical and condescending ways aren't going to improve your position there one bit.
MSG.11: Read it for yourself in Scripture: "As I live and breathe," God says, "every knee will bow before me; Every tongue will tell the honest truth that I and only I am God."
MSG.12: So tend to your knitting. You've got your hands full just taking care of your own life before God.
MSG.13: Forget about deciding what's right for each other. Here's what you need to be concerned about: that you don't get in the way of someone else, making life more difficult than it already is.
MSG.14: I'm convinced—Jesus convinced me!—that everything as it is in itself is holy. We, of course, by the way we treat it or talk about it, can contaminate it.
MSG.15: If you confuse others by making a big issue over what they eat or don't eat, you're no longer a companion with them in love, are you? These, remember, are persons for whom Christ died. Would you risk sending them to hell over an item in their diet?
MSG.16: Don't you dare let a piece of God-blessed food become an occasion of soul-poisoning!
MSG.17: God's kingdom isn't a matter of what you put in your stomach, for goodness' sake. It's what God does with your life as he sets it right, puts it together, and completes it with joy.
MSG.18: Your task is to single-mindedly serve Christ. Do that and you'll kill two birds with one stone: pleasing the God above you and proving your worth to the people around you.
MSG.19: So let's agree to use all our energy in getting along with each other. Help others with encouraging words;
MSG.20: don't drag them down by finding fault. You're certainly not going to permit an argument over what is served or not served at supper to wreck God's work among you, are you? I said it before and I'll say it again: All food is good, but it can turn bad if you use it badly, if you use it to trip others up and send them sprawling.
MSG.21: When you sit down to a meal, your primary concern should not be to feed your own face but to share the life of Jesus. So be sensitive and courteous to the others who are eating. Don't eat or say or do things that might interfere with the free exchange of love.
MSG.22: Cultivate your own relationship with God, but don't impose it on others. You're fortunate if your behavior and your belief are coherent.
MSG.23: But if you're not sure, if you notice that you are acting in ways inconsistent with what you believe—some days trying to impose your opinions on others, other days just trying to please them—then you know that you're out of line. If the way you live isn't consistent with what you believe, then it's wrong.

NIV.1:
The Weak and the Strong
Accept him whose faith is weak, without passing judgment on disputable matters.
NIV.2: One man’s faith allows him to eat everything, but another man, whose faith is weak, eats only vegetables.
NIV.3: The man who eats everything must not look down on him who does not, and the man who does not eat everything must not condemn the man who does, for God has accepted him.
NIV.4: Who are you to judge someone else’s servant? To his own master he stands or falls. And he will stand, for the Lord is able to make him stand.
NIV.5: One man considers one day more sacred than another; another man considers every day alike. Each one should be fully convinced in his own mind.
NIV.6: He who regards one day as special, does so to the Lord. He who eats meat, eats to the Lord, for he gives thanks to God; and he who abstains, does so to the Lord and gives thanks to God.
NIV.7: For none of us lives to himself alone and none of us dies to himself alone.
NIV.8: If we live, we live to the Lord; and if we die, we die to the Lord. So, whether we live or die, we belong to the Lord.
NIV.9: For this very reason, Christ died and returned to life so that he might be the Lord of both the dead and the living.
NIV.10: You, then, why do you judge your brother? Or why do you look down on your brother? For we will all stand before God’s judgment seat.
NIV.11:
It is written:
“‘As surely as I live,’ says the Lord,
‘every knee will bow before me;
every tongue will confess to God.’”
NIV.12: So then, each of us will give an account of himself to God.
NIV.13: Therefore let us stop passing judgment on one another. Instead, make up your mind not to put any stumbling block or obstacle in your brother’s way.
NIV.14: As one who is in the Lord Jesus, I am fully convinced that no food is unclean in itself. But if anyone regards something as unclean, then for him it is unclean.
NIV.15: If your brother is distressed because of what you eat, you are no longer acting in love. Do not by your eating destroy your brother for whom Christ died.
NIV.16: Do not allow what you consider good to be spoken of as evil.
NIV.17: For the kingdom of God is not a matter of eating and drinking, but of righteousness, peace and joy in the Holy Spirit,
NIV.18: because anyone who serves Christ in this way is pleasing to God and approved by men.
NIV.19: Let us therefore make every effort to do what leads to peace and to mutual edification.
NIV.20: Do not destroy the work of God for the sake of food. All food is clean, but it is wrong for a man to eat anything that causes someone else to stumble.
NIV.21: It is better not to eat meat or drink wine or to do anything else that will cause your brother to fall.
NIV.22: So whatever you believe about these things keep between yourself and God. Blessed is the man who does not condemn himself by what he approves.
NIV.23: But the man who has doubts is condemned if he eats, because his eating is not from faith; and everything that does not come from faith is sin.

NRSVCE.1: Welcome those who are weak in faith, but not for the purpose of quarreling over opinions.
NRSVCE.2: Some believe in eating anything, while the weak eat only vegetables.
NRSVCE.3: Those who eat must not despise those who abstain, and those who abstain must not pass judgment on those who eat; for God has welcomed them.
NRSVCE.4: Who are you to pass judgment on servants of another? It is before their own lord that they stand or fall. And they will be upheld, for the Lord is able to make them stand.
NRSVCE.5: Some judge one day to be better than another, while others judge all days to be alike. Let all be fully convinced in their own minds.
NRSVCE.6: Those who observe the day, observe it in honor of the Lord. Also those who eat, eat in honor of the Lord, since they give thanks to God; while those who abstain, abstain in honor of the Lord and give thanks to God.
NRSVCE.7: We do not live to ourselves, and we do not die to ourselves.
NRSVCE.8: If we live, we live to the Lord, and if we die, we die to the Lord; so then, whether we live or whether we die, we are the Lord’s.
NRSVCE.9: For to this end Christ died and lived again, so that he might be Lord of both the dead and the living.
NRSVCE.10: Why do you pass judgment on your brother or sister? Or you, why do you despise your brother or sister? For we will all stand before the judgment seat of God.
NRSVCE.11:
For it is written, “As I live, says the Lord, every knee shall bow to me,
and every tongue shall give praise to God.”
NRSVCE.12: So then, each of us will be accountable to God.
NRSVCE.13: Let us therefore no longer pass judgment on one another, but resolve instead never to put a stumbling block or hindrance in the way of another.
NRSVCE.14: I know and am persuaded in the Lord Jesus that nothing is unclean in itself; but it is unclean for anyone who thinks it unclean.
NRSVCE.15: If your brother or sister is being injured by what you eat, you are no longer walking in love. Do not let what you eat cause the ruin of one for whom Christ died.
NRSVCE.16: So do not let your good be spoken of as evil.
NRSVCE.17: For the kingdom of God is not food and drink but righteousness and peace and joy in the Holy Spirit.
NRSVCE.18: The one who thus serves Christ is acceptable to God and has human approval.
NRSVCE.19: Let us then pursue what makes for peace and for mutual upbuilding.
NRSVCE.20: Do not, for the sake of food, destroy the work of God. Everything is indeed clean, but it is wrong for you to make others fall by what you eat;
NRSVCE.21: it is good not to eat meat or drink wine or do anything that makes your brother or sister stumble.
NRSVCE.22: The faith that you have, have as your own conviction before God. Blessed are those who have no reason to condemn themselves because of what they approve.
NRSVCE.23: But those who have doubts are condemned if they eat, because they do not act from faith; for whatever does not proceed from faith is sin.

NET.1: Now receive the one who is weak in the faith, and do not have disputes over differing opinions.
NET.2: One person believes in eating everything, but the weak person eats only vegetables.
NET.3: The one who eats everything must not despise the one who does not, and the one who abstains must not judge the one who eats everything, for God has accepted him.
NET.4: Who are you to pass judgment on another’s servant? Before his own master he stands or falls. And he will stand, for the Lord is able to make him stand.
NET.5: One person regards one day holier than other days, and another regards them all alike. Each must be fully convinced in his own mind.
NET.6: The one who observes the day does it for the Lord. The one who eats, eats for the Lord because he gives thanks to God, and the one who abstains from eating abstains for the Lord, and he gives thanks to God.
NET.7: For none of us lives for himself and none dies for himself.
NET.8: If we live, we live for the Lord; if we die, we die for the Lord. Therefore, whether we live or die, we are the Lord’s.
NET.9: For this reason Christ died and returned to life, so that he may be the Lord of both the dead and the living.
NET.10: But you who eat vegetables only — why do you judge your brother or sister? And you who eat everything — why do you despise your brother or sister? For we will all stand before the judgment seat of God.
NET.11: For it is written, “As I live, says the Lord, every knee will bow to me, and every tongue will give praise to God.”
NET.12: Therefore, each of us will give an account of himself to God.
NET.13: Therefore we must not pass judgment on one another, but rather determine never to place an obstacle or a trap before a brother or sister.
NET.14: I know and am convinced in the Lord Jesus that there is nothing unclean in itself; still, it is unclean to the one who considers it unclean.
NET.15: For if your brother or sister is distressed because of what you eat, you are no longer walking in love. Do not destroy by your food someone for whom Christ died.
NET.16: Therefore do not let what you consider good be spoken of as evil.
NET.17: For the kingdom of God does not consist of food and drink, but righteousness, peace, and joy in the Holy Spirit.
NET.18: For the one who serves Christ in this way is pleasing to God and approved by people.
NET.19: So then, let us pursue what makes for peace and for building up one another.
NET.20: Do not destroy the work of God for the sake of food. For although all things are clean, it is wrong to cause anyone to stumble by what you eat.
NET.21: It is good not to eat meat or drink wine or to do anything that causes your brother to stumble.
NET.22: The faith you have, keep to yourself before God. Blessed is the one who does not judge himself by what he approves.
NET.23: But the man who doubts is condemned if he eats, because he does not do so from faith, and whatever is not from faith is sin.
